content:
{
  "title": "Chain-of-Restraint Prompting: Sequential Constraint Injection for Robust LLM Defense",
  "problem": "Current prompting techniques for LLMs are vulnerable to adversarial attacks and jailbreak attempts that circumvent safety constraints. Existing methods typically apply static restrictions or guardrails, which attackers can often bypass.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs are increasingly deployed in sensitive applications, their susceptibility to adversarial prompting poses real risks. The rapid evolution of jailbreak strategies demands adaptive defense mechanisms that generalize beyond known attacks. Improving robustness at the prompt level could provide a lightweight, model-agnostic layer of protection without retraining.",
  "method": "We propose 'Chain-of-Restraint Prompting' (CoRP), a method that interleaves task instructions with a dynamically generated sequence of constraint-check prompts. For every primary user query, the system recursively synthesizes auxiliary prompts that ask the model to self-assess, critique, and refine its own output based on adaptive safety rubrics informed by external attack taxonomies. These constraint-check steps are inserted as intermediate turns and aggregate their outputs to inform the final response. CoRP is model-agnostic, requiring no access to internal weights or training data, and can be implemented on top of any LLM API.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will test CoRP on public jailbreak/adversarial prompt datasets (e.g., AdvBench, JailbreakBench) using several open- and closed-source LLMs. Baselines will include single-turn system prompts, static guardrails, and existing multi-turn self-reflection techniques. Metrics will measure attack success rate, helpfulness, and task completion. Human and automated evaluation will jointly assess both robustness and utility."
}
